'prime' = meets all specifications for production; 'test' = test wafers used for process qualification; 'engineering' = engineering samples not for production; 'monitor' = process monitor wafers; 'reject' = failed quality inspection and not usable.

Die or wafer bin classification code assigned during wafer sort or final test, indicating the performance grade or speed bin of the die (e.g., 'BIN1_HIGH_PERF', 'BIN2_STD', 'BIN3_LOW_SPEED', 'REJECT'). Used for die bank management, customer order fulfillment by grade, and yield analysis. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: promote to reference product as bin classifications vary by product family]

Identifier of the Outsourced Semiconductor Assembly and Test (OSAT) partner or external consignee at whose facility this consignment stock is physically held. Populated only for stock_type = 'consignment'. Used for consignment reconciliation and OSAT partner performance tracking.

Indicates whether this material is subject to export control regulations (True), including ITAR (International Traffic in Arms Regulations) or EAR (Export Administration Regulations) administered by BIS. Export-controlled inventory requires additional authorization before transfer to foreign nationals or shipment to controlled countries.

Indicates whether this material is classified as hazardous (True) under applicable regulations (OSHA HazCom, REACH, TSCA, or DOT). Hazardous materials include certain process chemicals, gases (e.g., arsine, phosphine), and solvents used in semiconductor fabrication. Triggers special storage, handling, and disposal requirements.

Indicates the Known Good Die (KGD) qualification status for die bank inventory. 'known_good' = die has passed all electrical and functional tests; 'failed' = die has failed one or more tests; 'untested' = die has not yet been tested; 'conditionally_good' = die passed with minor deviations within specification limits. Applicable only to die bank material types.

Date of the most recent goods issue (GI) posting for this material from this storage location. Used to identify dormant inventory, assess consumption velocity, and support slow-moving inventory analysis.

Date of the most recent goods receipt (GR) posting for this material at this storage location. Used to calculate stock aging, assess replenishment lead times, and support FIFO/FEFO inventory consumption sequencing.

Date of the most recent physical inventory count or cycle count performed for this material at this storage location. Used to assess inventory accuracy, schedule next count, and support SOX financial reporting requirements for inventory valuation.

The human-readable lot or batch identifier assigned by the MES (Camstar or Applied Materials SmartFactory) for WIP wafer lots, or by the ERP for raw material and finished goods batches. Critical for traceability, yield analysis, and IATF 16949 compliance.

Moisture Sensitivity Level (MSL) classification per IPC/JEDEC J-STD-020 for packaged semiconductor devices and components. Determines floor life limits and baking requirements before reflow soldering. Applicable to finished goods and packaging inventory. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: 1|2|2a|3|4|5|5a|6 — 8 candidates stripped; promote to reference product]

Quantity of unrestricted stock that is not committed to any open reservation or production order. Represents the net available quantity for Available-to-Promise (ATP) calculations and new production planning decisions. Computed as unrestricted stock minus open reservations.

Quantity of material placed in blocked stock due to quality rejection, ITAR/EAR export control restrictions, supplier non-conformance, or regulatory hold. Blocked stock is not available for production or shipment until formally released.

Quantity of material that has been shipped from a source location but has not yet been received at the destination location. Includes inter-plant transfers, OSAT shipments, and inbound supplier deliveries in transit.

Quantity of material currently consumed into active production orders and undergoing fabrication processing (e.g., wafer lots in FEOL/BEOL processing, die in packaging). Tracked by the MES and reflected in SAP PP WIP accounts.

Total physical quantity of the material present at the storage location across all stock types, expressed in the base unit of measure. Represents the gross inventory position before reservations or quality holds are considered.

Quantity of material currently held in SAP QM quality inspection stock, pending disposition decision (accept, reject, rework). Includes incoming inspection lots, in-process inspection holds, and post-process quality holds.

Total quantity committed to active inventory reservations linked to open production orders, sales orders, or transfer orders. Corresponds to open inventory_reservation records and reduces the available quantity for new demand.
Timestamp when this stock balance record was first created in the lakehouse silver layer. Supports audit trail requirements for SOX financial reporting and data governance lineage tracking.

Timestamp when this stock balance record was last updated in the lakehouse silver layer, reflecting the most recent refresh from the source system. Used for incremental load processing and data freshness monitoring.

The minimum on-hand quantity threshold at which a replenishment order should be triggered for this material at this storage location. Maintained in SAP MRP parameters and used by production planning to prevent stockouts of critical raw materials, chemicals, and gases.

Indicates whether this material lot is compliant with the EU RoHS Directive (Restriction of Hazardous Substances), restricting the use of lead, mercury, cadmium, hexavalent chromium, PBB, and PBDE. Required for finished goods destined for EU markets and tracked per Oracle Agile PLM compliance records.

Buffer stock quantity maintained above the reorder point to protect against demand variability and supply lead time uncertainty. Defined in SAP MRP safety stock parameters. Critical for high-value materials like EUV photomasks and specialty process gases with long procurement lead times.

Date on which the material's shelf life expires and it becomes unusable for production. Applicable to chemicals, photoresists, gases, and certain packaging materials. Enforced by SAP batch management shelf-life expiration date (SLED) functionality. Materials past this date must be quarantined and disposed per REACH and RoHS regulations.

Indicates whether this stock balance has been flagged as slow-moving (True) based on the stock_aging_days exceeding the material-specific slow-moving threshold. Used for inventory write-down risk assessment, EOL (End of Life) planning, and LTB (Last Time Buy) decision support.

The principal business event timestamp indicating when this stock balance snapshot was captured from the source system (SAP MM or MES). Represents the point-in-time inventory position and is the primary temporal key for real-time inventory visibility and ATP calculations.

Identifies the operational system of record from which this stock balance record was sourced. Enables data lineage tracking and reconciliation across SAP MM, Camstar MES, Applied Materials SmartFactory MES, and Oracle Agile PLM in the lakehouse silver layer.

SAP special stock indicator identifying non-standard stock ownership or custody arrangements. 'E' = sales order stock; 'K' = consignment stock at customer; 'Q' = project stock; 'W' = consignment stock from vendor; 'O' = stock at subcontractor (OSAT). Empty value indicates standard own stock.

Number of days the current on-hand stock has been held at this storage location since the oldest receipt date. Used for slow-moving inventory identification, shelf-life monitoring, and obsolescence risk assessment. Critical for chemicals, gases, and photoresists with defined shelf lives.

SAP stock category indicating the usability status of the inventory. 'unrestricted' is available for production or shipment; 'quality_inspection' is under QM hold pending disposition; 'blocked' is rejected or quarantined; 'consignment' is held at OSAT partner sites; 'restricted' is subject to export control or ITAR/EAR restrictions.

Required storage environment classification for this material. Drives warehouse management system (WMS) slotting and compliance with SEMI, REACH, and OSHA storage requirements. 'nitrogen_purge' for moisture-sensitive devices (MSD); 'cold_storage' for temperature-sensitive chemicals; 'cleanroom_class1/10' for wafers and photomasks; 'hazmat' for regulated chemicals.

Base unit of measure for all quantity fields on this stock balance record. 'EA' = each (packaged chips), 'WFR' = wafer, 'LOT' = wafer lot, 'KG' = kilogram (chemicals), 'L' = liter (liquid chemicals/gases), 'M2' = square meter (substrate), 'MASK' = photomask, 'DIE' = individual die. Aligned with SAP base UoM configuration. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: EA|WFR|LOT|KG|L|M2|MASK|DIE — 8 candidates stripped; promote to reference product]

Indicates whether this stock balance record represents inventory that is fully available for unrestricted use in production or shipment (True), or whether it is subject to any hold, restriction, or quality disposition (False). Derived from stock_type but maintained as an explicit flag for rapid ATP query performance.

SAP valuation class code that determines the general ledger (GL) accounts to which inventory postings are made for this material. Links inventory movements to the correct balance sheet accounts for financial reporting. Detailed financial valuation fields are maintained in the stock_valuation data product.

The semiconductor process technology node at which the wafer lot or die was fabricated (e.g., '7nm', '14nm', '28nm', '180nm'). Critical for inventory segregation, process compatibility checks, and customer design-in alignment.
Unique surrogate identifier for the storage location master record within the inventory management system. Primary key for the storage_location entity.

Foreign key linking to workforce.employee. Business justification: Storage Location Management assigns a manager responsible for safety, access control, and inventory integrity, used in location audit reports.

Physical access control level for this storage location. unrestricted — open access; badge-access — requires valid facility badge; authorized-personnel — restricted to named personnel list (e.g., for ITAR-controlled materials); security-vault — highest security for KGD die banks, photomask vaults, and ITAR-controlled IP. Supports ITAR/EAR export control compliance.

Bay designation within the building or cleanroom where this storage location is physically situated (e.g., 'BAY-03', 'A-WEST'). Used for precise physical navigation, equipment layout planning, and cleanroom zone management per SEMI E187.

Identifier for the physical building within the facility where this storage location resides (e.g., 'FAB1', 'CHEM-WH-B', 'OSAT-PKG-C'). Supports facility layout mapping, emergency response planning, and environmental zone management.

Unit of measure used to express the maximum capacity and current utilization of this storage location. Varies by facility type: cassette (wafer storage), pod (photomask storage), tray (packaged chip storage), container (chemical storage), box (die bank storage), pallet (bulk warehouse storage).

ISO 14644-1 cleanroom classification for this storage location, defining the maximum allowable airborne particulate concentration. Critical for wafer, photomask, and KGD die storage compliance. Applicable only to cleanroom facility types; null for non-cleanroom locations. Values: ISO-1 (cleanest) through ISO-6.

Date on which this storage location was formally commissioned and made available for inventory use. Marks the start of the location's operational lifecycle. Used for asset age tracking, depreciation scheduling, and facility capacity planning.

Three-letter ISO 3166-1 alpha-3 country code of the country where this storage location is physically situated (e.g., 'USA', 'TWN', 'KOR', 'JPN'). Critical for ITAR/EAR export control jurisdiction determination, REACH/RoHS regulatory applicability, and multi-national inventory reporting.

Timestamp when this storage location master record was first created in the system (ISO 8601 format: yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ss.SSSXXX). Provides audit trail for record provenance and data governance compliance per SOX and ISO 9001 quality management requirements.

Current number of inventory units occupying this storage location at the time of last inventory update. Compared against max_capacity_units to compute utilization percentage for capacity planning and inventory placement decisions. Updated by MES and SAP MM inventory movements.

Date on which this storage location was permanently decommissioned and removed from active inventory use. Populated only when location_status is 'decommissioned'. Supports facility lifecycle management and historical inventory traceability.

ESD protection classification of this storage location per ANSI/ESD S20.20 and JEDEC JESD625 standards. Determines suitability for storing ESD-sensitive devices (ICs, dies, packaged chips). class-0 (most sensitive, <250V HBM) through class-3 (>2000V HBM); unprotected for non-ESD-controlled areas.

Classification of the physical facility type housing this storage location. Drives environmental compliance rules, MSD floor life management, and hazmat handling procedures. Valid values: cleanroom (ISO-classified FAB area), warehouse (general materials storage), cold-storage (temperature-controlled for chemicals/biologics), gas-cabinet (pressurized specialty gas storage), nitrogen-cabinet (nitrogen-purged dry storage for moisture-sensitive devices), die-vault (secure KGD die bank storage).

Type of fire suppression system installed at this storage location. Critical for chemical warehouse and gas cabinet locations storing flammable or reactive materials. Drives emergency response planning and insurance compliance. Values: sprinkler, halon (legacy), fm200 (clean agent), co2, dry-powder, none.

Geographic latitude coordinate (WGS84 decimal degrees) of the facility where this storage location resides. Used for multi-site supply chain mapping, emergency response planning, and regulatory jurisdiction determination for ITAR/EAR export control compliance.

Geographic longitude coordinate (WGS84 decimal degrees) of the facility where this storage location resides. Used in conjunction with geo_latitude for multi-site supply chain mapping and regulatory jurisdiction determination.

UN/DOT hazardous materials classification code applicable to materials stored at this location (e.g., 'UN1170' for ethanol, 'UN2014' for hydrogen peroxide, 'FLAMMABLE-LIQUID', 'CORROSIVE', 'COMPRESSED-GAS', 'NON-HAZMAT'). Governs storage segregation, emergency response, and regulatory reporting under REACH, RoHS, and TSCA. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: flammable-liquid|corrosive|compressed-gas|oxidizer|toxic|non-hazmat|pyrophoric — promote to reference product]

Inventory valuation method applied to stock held at this storage location for financial reporting purposes. standard-cost (used for WIP wafer lots), moving-average (raw materials), fifo (finished goods), fefo (shelf-life-managed chemicals). Aligns with SAP S/4HANA FI/CO valuation area configuration and SOX financial reporting requirements.

business_glossary_term: Outsourced Semiconductor Assembly and Test (OSAT) Partner Location Flag
description: Indicates whether this storage location is physically situated at an OSAT (Outsourced Semiconductor Assembly and Test) partner facility rather than an internal company site. True for consignment stock locations at OSAT partners. Drives consignment inventory valuation, ownership tracking, and inter-company transfer accounting.

business_glossary_term: International Traffic in Arms Regulations (ITAR) Controlled Flag
description: Indicates whether this storage location is designated for ITAR-controlled materials, requiring strict access logging, personnel authorization, and regulatory reporting. True for locations storing defense-related ICs, ASIC designs, or photomasks subject to ITAR jurisdiction. Drives export control compliance workflows.

business_glossary_term: Known Good Die (KGD) Storage Certified Flag
description: Indicates whether this storage location is certified for KGD (Known Good Die) die bank storage, meeting requirements for ESD protection, nitrogen purge, temperature/humidity control, and access security. KGD storage certification is required for high-value tested dies awaiting packaging or shipment.

Date of the most recent environmental and safety inspection conducted for this storage location. Used to track compliance with SEMI S2, ISO 14001, and ISO 45001 periodic inspection requirements. Drives inspection scheduling and regulatory audit readiness.

Timestamp of the most recent modification to this storage location master record (ISO 8601 format: yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ss.SSSXXX). Used for change data capture (CDC), data synchronization between SAP S/4HANA and the Databricks Silver Layer, and audit trail maintenance.

Externally-known alphanumeric code uniquely identifying the storage location, aligned with SAP S/4HANA MM storage location key (LGORT) and Camstar MES location identifiers. Used for lot traceability, WIP tracking, and inventory placement across FAB, warehouse, and OSAT partner facilities.

Human-readable descriptive name of the storage location (e.g., 'Bay 3 Shelf 12 — EUV Photomask Vault', 'Cleanroom A Chemical Cabinet Row 5'). Used in operational reports, MES screens, and inventory placement instructions.

Current operational lifecycle status of the storage location. 'active' — available for inventory placement; 'blocked' — temporarily unavailable due to maintenance, contamination, or audit hold; 'decommissioned' — permanently retired from use. Controls whether new inventory can be assigned to this location.

Maximum number of inventory units (wafer cassettes, chemical containers, photomask pods, chip trays, or die boxes) that can be physically stored at this location. Used for capacity planning, inventory placement optimization, and utilization reporting.

Maximum allowable relative humidity percentage (0–100%) for this storage location. Exceedance above maximum triggers MSD floor life reset, inventory quarantine, and environmental non-conformance reporting per IPC/JEDEC J-STD-033.

Maximum allowable storage temperature in degrees Celsius for this location. Defines the upper bound of the acceptable temperature range. Exceedance triggers inventory quarantine and environmental non-conformance reporting per ISO 14001 and SEMI C10 chemical storage guidelines.

Minimum allowable relative humidity percentage (0–100%) for this storage location. Critical for MSD floor life management, photomask storage, and chemical stability. Exceedance below minimum may cause ESD risk or material degradation.

Minimum allowable storage temperature in degrees Celsius for this location. Defines the lower bound of the acceptable temperature range for stored materials (chemicals, photoresists, gases, packaged devices). Used for environmental compliance monitoring and MSD (Moisture Sensitive Device) floor life management per IPC/JEDEC J-STD-033.

business_glossary_term: Moisture Sensitive Device (MSD) Floor Life Management Capable Flag
description: Indicates whether this storage location is equipped and certified to manage MSD floor life tracking per IPC/JEDEC J-STD-033. True for nitrogen-purged cabinets, dry-storage cabinets, and controlled-humidity cleanroom locations. Enables automatic floor life clock suspension for stored MSDs.

Maximum Moisture Sensitivity Level (MSL) of devices that can be safely stored at this location per IPC/JEDEC J-STD-020. MSL-1 (unlimited floor life at ≤30°C/85%RH) through MSL-5 (most sensitive, 24-hour floor life). Determines which packaged semiconductor devices are eligible for placement at this location.

Scheduled date for the next mandatory environmental and safety inspection of this storage location. Automatically calculated based on inspection frequency requirements per SEMI S2 and ISO 14001. Triggers inspection work orders in the facility management system.

Indicates whether this storage location is equipped with nitrogen purge capability to maintain an inert, moisture-free atmosphere. True for nitrogen-cabinet and die-vault locations used for long-term KGD die storage, photomask preservation, and moisture-sensitive chemical storage. Drives MSD floor life management decisions.

business_glossary_term: Outsourced Semiconductor Assembly and Test (OSAT) Partner Name
description: Name of the OSAT partner company at whose facility this storage location resides (e.g., 'ASE Group', 'Amkor Technology', 'JCET'). Populated only when is_osat_partner_location is True. Used for consignment stock management, partner performance reporting, and supply chain visibility.

Indicates whether this storage location meets the environmental and cleanliness requirements for photomask (reticle) storage, including ISO cleanroom class, nitrogen purge, and ESD protection. Photomasks are high-value assets used in EUV/DUV lithography and require specialized storage conditions.

SAP S/4HANA plant identifier to which this storage location belongs. Represents the manufacturing or distribution facility (FAB, OSAT partner site, or warehouse) in the enterprise organizational hierarchy. Enables multi-plant inventory management and inter-plant transfer tracking.

Row designation within the bay for this storage location (e.g., 'R-12', 'ROW-A'). Provides granular physical addressing for inventory placement, pick-and-put-away operations, and automated storage/retrieval system (ASRS) integration.

Shelf or bin designation within the row for this storage location (e.g., 'S-04', 'BIN-002'). Lowest level of the physical location hierarchy, enabling precise inventory placement and retrieval for wafer cassettes, chemical containers, photomasks, and packaged chip trays.

Indicates whether shelf life expiry tracking is enabled for materials stored at this location. True for chemical storage, photoresist storage, and gas cabinet locations where materials have defined shelf lives. Enables automated expiry alerts and FEFO (First Expired First Out) inventory management.

Maximum allowable total weight in kilograms for materials stored at this location. Critical for shelf structural integrity compliance, particularly for heavy chemical drums, gas cylinders, and bulk wafer cassette storage. Enforced per facility safety standards.

business_glossary_term: Work in Process (WIP) Staging Area Flag
description: Indicates whether this storage location is designated as a WIP (Work in Process) staging area for wafer lots between process steps. True for inter-bay buffers, equipment load ports, and process queue areas within the FAB cleanroom. Enables WIP flow tracking and cycle time analysis in the MES.
Unique system-generated identifier for the assembly lot record.

External order number from the customer that triggered this assembly lot.

Foreign key linking to fabrication.fabrication_wafer_lot. Business justification: Lot Traceability Report links each packaging assembly lot to its originating wafer lot for yield, compliance, and audit tracking.
Foreign key linking to product.ic_catalog. Business justification: Yield and defect analysis per IC design needs the lot to reference its IC catalog entry.
Foreign key linking to design.ic_design_project. Business justification: Lot tracking reports require the originating design project to correlate yield and defect data with design intent.

Foreign key linking to quality.inspection_lot. Business justification: Supports Lot Traceability workflow linking each assembly lot to the preceding inspection lot for defect tracking across packaging.
Foreign key linking to workforce.employee. Business justification: Lot manager is recorded for WIP ownership, yield accountability, and shift hand‑over documentation.

Foreign key linking to packaging.packaging_line. Business justification: An assembly lot is processed on a specific packaging line; linking the lot to the line provides traceability of line usage for yield and defect analysis.

FK to packaging.assembly_order.assembly_order_id — Assembly lots are created under an assembly order. This is the primary parent-child relationship for WIP tracking.
FK to packaging.osat_vendor.osat_vendor_id — Each lot is processed at a specific OSAT site. Required for site-level yield analysis and capacity tracking.
Foreign key linking to process.process_process_flow. Business justification: REQUIRED: Each assembly lot originates from a wafer lot produced by a specific fab flow; needed for traceability.

Foreign key linking to finance.profit_center. Business justification: Profit Center Performance report allocates revenue and profit per packaging lot to the profit center responsible for the product line.
Foreign key linking to product.sku. Business justification: Lot‑level tracking report requires linking each assembly lot to the SKU it contains.
Foreign key linking to inventory.storage_location. Business justification: Logistics and Inventory Valuation require storing the location of each assembled package for warehouse management.

The most recent manufacturing step the lot has completed or is currently executing.
Number of defects per unit area (e.g., defects per cm²) observed on the lot.
Total number of individual dies contained in the assembly lot.
Indicates whether the lot is currently on hold for any reason.

Number of inspection points that failed during the latest quality check.
Number of inspection points that passed during the latest quality check.

Human‑readable name or label for the assembly lot, often used in reports and dashboards.
External business identifier assigned to the assembly lot, used for tracking across systems.
FK to packaging.packaging_package_type.packaging_package_type_id — Each assembly lot is for a specific package type. Required for WIP reporting and routing.
Overall lifecycle state of the lot, indicating whether it is active, completed, or terminated.
FK to packaging.pkg_assembly_order.pkg_assembly_order_id — Assembly lots are created against assembly orders. This is the fundamental WIP-to-order traceability link.
Version or revision identifier of the package design used for this lot.
Result of the most recent quality inspection for the lot.

FK to packaging.package_type.package_type_id — Every assembly order specifies a package type — this is the primary classification that determines process flow, substrate, and equipment. Critical for filtering orders by package family.

Foreign key linking to finance.cost_center. Business justification: Assembly Order Cost Tracking report requires associating each order with the responsible cost center for budgeting and expense allocation.

Foreign key linking to customer.customer_design_win. Business justification: Required for linking each assembly order to the specific customer design win that authorizes production, used in the Order Fulfillment process.
Foreign key linking to inventory.die_bank. Business justification: Needed for Assembly Order Cost Allocation and Regulatory Traceability linking the order to the die bank supplying the dies.

Foreign key linking to compliance.eccn_classification. Business justification: ECCN classification determines export control category for each order; needed for compliance reporting and customs filings.

Foreign key linking to compliance.export_license. Business justification: Required for Export License Verification Report; each assembly order must be linked to the export license governing its shipment.
Identifier of the primary equipment used for the assembly.

Foreign key linking to design.ic_design_project. Business justification: Assembly Order is created for a specific IC design project; linking enables traceability, cost allocation, and schedule alignment in the packaging execution process.
Identifier of the substrate lot used in the packaging process.

Foreign key linking to design.mpw_shuttle. Business justification: MPW shuttle schedule drives packaging lead times; linking the order to the shuttle provides coordinated planning across design and packaging.
Foreign key linking to workforce.employee. Business justification: Sales order assignment to a sales rep is used in order‑to‑cash reporting and commission calculations.
Identifier of the specific packaging line used.
Identifier of the outsourced assembly partner responsible for execution.

FK to packaging.assembly_process_flow.assembly_process_flow_id — Each assembly order executes against a specific process flow revision. Required for traceability and change control.

Foreign key linking to supply.purchase_order. Business justification: REQUIRED: Assembly order fulfillment depends on a purchase order; linking supports order‑PO status reconciliation and financial reporting.
Foreign key linking to sales.quote. Business justification: Assembly orders are created from approved sales quotes; linking provides traceability from quote to manufacturing.

List of customer names or identifiers who have products affected by this change and require notification per contractual agreements.
List of manufacturing equipment tools or tool sets impacted by this process change, including tool IDs or model numbers.
List of raw materials, chemicals, gases, or consumables that are being changed or affected by this process modification.
Comma-separated list or description of product families, part numbers, or device types impacted by this process change.
Date when the process change notification received final internal approval to proceed.
Internal approval status from engineering, quality, and management stakeholders for proceeding with the process change.
Name or identifier of the person or role who provided final approval for the process change notification.
References to supporting documentation such as technical specifications, test reports, FMEA analyses, customer communications, or qualification data associated with this PCN.

Impact classification of the change: major (requires customer approval and qualification), minor (notification required but no requalification), or notification_only (informational, no customer action required).
Detailed description of the process change including what is being changed, why the change is necessary, and the technical details of the modification.
Business or technical justification for the change, such as yield improvement, cost reduction, obsolescence mitigation, reliability enhancement, or regulatory compliance.

Current lifecycle status of the process change notification in the approval and implementation workflow. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: draft|pending_approval|approved|customer_notified|implemented|cancelled|rejected — 7 candidates stripped; promote to reference product]
Brief descriptive title summarizing the nature of the process change.

Category of change being notified: process (recipe or flow modification), material (raw material or chemical change), equipment (tool or configuration change), design (mask or layout change), facility (fab site or cleanroom change), or supplier (vendor or subcontractor change).
Assessment of the financial impact of the process change including implementation costs, ongoing cost savings or increases, and return on investment.
Timestamp when the process change notification record was first created in the system.
Indicates whether explicit customer approval is required before implementing the change, typically for major changes affecting form, fit, function, or reliability.
Indicates whether customer notification is required per contractual agreements, regulatory requirements, or change impact assessment.
Expected change in manufacturing cycle time resulting from the process change, measured in hours (positive for increase, negative for reduction).
Description of any environmental, health, or safety impacts resulting from the process change, including changes to hazardous material usage or waste generation.
Code identifying the fabrication facility or site where the process change will be implemented.
Timestamp when the process change notification record was last updated or modified.
Final date for customers to place orders for products manufactured using the old process before the change is implemented.
Final date for shipping products manufactured using the old process before transitioning to the new process.
Additional comments, observations, or context related to the process change notification.
Date when affected customers were formally notified of the pending process change.
Name of the process engineer or technical owner responsible for managing this change notification through implementation.
Department or organizational unit responsible for the process change, such as Process Engineering, Equipment Engineering, or Quality Assurance.
Business identifier for the process change notification, typically formatted as PCN-NNNNNN. Used for external communication and tracking.
Target date for implementing the process change in production manufacturing.
Date when all qualification and validation activities for the process change were successfully completed.
Status of the qualification and validation activities required to verify that the process change does not adversely affect product quality or reliability.
Assessment of whether the process change affects compliance with regulatory requirements such as RoHS, REACH, ITAR, EAR, or automotive quality standards (IATF 16949).

Evaluation of potential risks associated with the change including impact on product performance, reliability, quality, and manufacturability. May reference Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) results.
Overall risk rating assigned to this change based on potential impact to product quality, reliability, and customer operations.
Expected or measured change in manufacturing yield resulting from the process change, expressed as a percentage point change (positive for improvement, negative for degradation).
Primary key for cooling_optimization_run
Self-referencing FK on cooling_optimization_run (baseline_cooling_optimization_run_id)

Foreign key linking to process.cooling_condition. Business justification: Cooling optimization runs test specific cooling conditions. This FK links the optimization run to the cooling condition being evaluated.

Foreign key linking to process.cooling_process. Business justification: Cooling optimization runs are conducted to optimize a specific cooling process. This FK links the optimization run to the cooling process being optimized.
Reference to the process engineer responsible for designing, executing, and analyzing this cooling optimization run. Used for accountability, knowledge management, and escalation routing.
Reference to the specific process equipment unit on which the cooling optimization run is conducted. Links to the equipment master for tool specifications and maintenance history.
Reference to the wafer lot processed during the cooling optimization run. Enables correlation of cooling parameters with lot-level yield and electrical performance outcomes.

Foreign key linking to process.process_flow. Business justification: Cooling optimization runs are conducted within a specific process flow context. This FK links the optimization run to the process flow it supports.

Foreign key linking to process.process_step. Business justification: Cooling optimization runs are conducted for a specific process step. This FK links the optimization run to the step being optimized.

Reference to the technology development or product program under which this cooling optimization run is conducted. Enables program-level aggregation of cooling optimization activities and resource tracking.
Reference to the process recipe used during the cooling optimization run. The recipe defines the baseline process parameters against which cooling modifications are evaluated.

Pressure of the backside cooling gas applied between the wafer and chuck, measured in Torr. Optimizing backside gas pressure improves wafer-to-chuck thermal conductance and wafer temperature uniformity.

Type of gas used for backside wafer cooling (typically helium) to enhance thermal contact between the wafer and chuck. Backside gas pressure and type are critical parameters in electrostatic chuck cooling optimization.
Wafer temperature measured under the pre-optimization (baseline) cooling configuration, in degrees Celsius. Provides the reference point against which optimization improvements are measured.
Iteration number within this run that yielded the optimal cooling performance result as defined by the optimization objective. Used to trace back to the specific parameter set that should be adopted.
Measured actual temperature of the chuck during the cooling optimization run, in degrees Celsius. Deviation from setpoint indicates cooling system performance and is a key optimization metric.

Target temperature setpoint for the electrostatic or mechanical chuck that holds the wafer during processing, in degrees Celsius. Chuck temperature directly controls wafer thermal uniformity and process outcomes.

Volumetric flow rate of the coolant during the optimization run, measured in liters per minute (LPM). A primary optimization variable controlling heat removal capacity from the process chamber or chuck.
Temperature of the coolant at the inlet to the cooling system during the run, measured in degrees Celsius. A key optimization parameter that sets the baseline thermal boundary condition.
Temperature of the coolant at the outlet of the cooling system during the run, measured in degrees Celsius. The delta between inlet and outlet temperatures quantifies heat absorbed from the process.
Operating pressure of the coolant circuit during the cooling optimization run, measured in kilopascals (kPa). Pressure affects flow rate, heat transfer efficiency, and system integrity.

Type of cooling medium used in the optimization run (e.g., deionized water, perfluorocarbon fluid, chilled water, nitrogen gas, helium gas). Determines heat transfer characteristics and compatibility with process chemistry.

Ratio of useful heat removed to total energy input for the cooling system during the run, expressed as a percentage. Measures the thermodynamic efficiency of the cooling configuration and supports energy optimization.
Date and time when this cooling optimization run record was first created in the system. Audit trail field for data lineage and compliance purposes.

Measured defect density on wafers processed during the cooling optimization run, expressed as defects per square centimeter. Used to correlate cooling parameter changes with defect generation and process quality.

Name or identifier of the Design of Experiment (DOE) design matrix used for this cooling optimization run (e.g., Box-Behnken, Central Composite, L9 Taguchi). Applicable when run_type is design_of_experiment.

Total electrical energy consumed by the cooling system during the optimization run, measured in kilowatt-hours (kWh). Supports energy efficiency analysis and sustainability reporting for fab operations.
Manufacturer model designation of the process equipment used in the cooling optimization run (e.g., ASML NXT:2000i, Applied Materials Centura). Supports equipment-specific parameter benchmarking.

Designated fabrication bay or cleanroom area within the fab where the cooling optimization run is executed (e.g., Bay 3A, Litho Bay, Diffusion Area). Used for spatial traceability and equipment grouping.

Rate at which heat is removed from the process chamber or wafer by the cooling system during the run, measured in Watts. Quantifies the effective cooling capacity achieved under the optimized parameters.

Free-text engineering notes capturing observations, anomalies, equipment issues, or contextual information relevant to this cooling optimization run. Supports knowledge transfer and root cause analysis.

Total number of parameter configuration iterations executed within this cooling optimization run. Tracks the breadth of the parameter space explored and the computational/experimental effort expended.

Statistical or computational method used to explore and optimize the cooling parameter space in this run (e.g., Design of Experiment full factorial, response surface methodology, gradient descent). Determines the experimental design and analysis approach.
Primary engineering objective driving this cooling optimization run. Defines the optimization criterion used to evaluate parameter configurations and select the optimal cooling setup.

Quantitative score representing the degree of optimization achieved in this run relative to the defined objective function. Higher scores indicate better optimization outcomes; used to compare runs and track improvement over time.
Wafer temperature achieved under the optimized cooling configuration identified by this run, in degrees Celsius. Compared against baseline to quantify the thermal improvement delivered.
Full name of the process engineer who owns and is accountable for this cooling optimization run. PII-tagged per VREQ-050; must be masked in non-production environments.

Process capability index (Cpk) calculated for the primary cooling parameter (e.g., chuck temperature) during this run. Cpk >= 1.33 is typically required for production qualification; used to assess cooling process stability.

Operating pressure inside the process chamber during the cooling optimization run, measured in millitorr (mTorr). Chamber pressure influences plasma density, etch/deposition rates, and thermal coupling.

Status of the formal qualification process for the optimized cooling parameters identified in this run. Tracks progression from initial optimization through engineering qualification to production release.
Version identifier of the process recipe used during this cooling optimization run. Ensures traceability to the exact recipe revision and supports change management.

Indicates whether the optimized cooling parameters identified in this run are recommended for adoption in production process recipes. True = parameters approved for production use; False = further optimization or validation required.

Radio frequency (RF) power applied to the process chamber during the run, measured in Watts. RF power is a primary heat source in plasma processes and directly determines the cooling load that must be managed.
Total elapsed time of the cooling optimization run from start to end, measured in minutes. Used for equipment scheduling, throughput analysis, and cycle time optimization.
Date and time when the cooling optimization run was completed or terminated. Used with run_start_timestamp to calculate total run duration and equipment utilization.
Externally-known business identifier for the cooling optimization run, used in engineering documentation, work orders, and cross-team communication. Format: COR-{YYYY}-{NNNNNN}.
Date and time when the cooling optimization run was initiated on the equipment. The principal business event timestamp marking the start of active experimentation.

Current lifecycle state of the cooling optimization run, tracking progression from planning through execution to completion or failure. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: planned|in_progress|completed|aborted|on_hold|failed — promote to reference product]

Classification of the cooling optimization run by its engineering purpose: baseline characterization, parametric sweep across cooling variables, design of experiment (DOE), validation of optimized parameters, or production monitoring run.

Indicates whether the cooling process parameters were within Statistical Process Control (SPC) control limits throughout the optimization run. True = all parameters in control; False = one or more SPC violations detected.

Semiconductor technology node (process generation) for which this cooling optimization run is performed, expressed in nanometers (e.g., 7nm, 5nm, 3nm). Determines thermal budget constraints and cooling requirements.

Reduction in wafer temperature achieved by the optimized cooling configuration compared to baseline, in degrees Celsius (baseline_wafer_temp_c minus optimized_wafer_temp_c). Key performance indicator for the optimization run.

Measured thermal resistance of the cooling interface between the chuck and the wafer, expressed in degrees Celsius per Watt (°C/W). Lower values indicate more efficient heat transfer and are a key optimization target.
Time required for the cooling system to reach thermal steady state at the target setpoint, measured in seconds. A key optimization metric; shorter stabilization times improve equipment throughput.
Date and time when this cooling optimization run record was last modified. Audit trail field supporting change tracking, data lineage, and incremental ETL processing.
Number of wafers included in the cooling optimization run. Determines thermal load and is used to validate cooling capacity under production-representative conditions.
Diameter of the wafers processed in this cooling optimization run, expressed in millimeters (e.g., 200, 300, 450). Wafer size directly impacts thermal mass and cooling system design requirements.

Measured peak-to-valley temperature variation across the wafer surface during the run, in degrees Celsius. A primary optimization objective; lower values indicate better thermal uniformity and improved process uniformity.

Estimated or measured change in wafer yield attributable to the cooling optimization, expressed as a percentage. Positive values indicate yield improvement; used to justify cooling optimization investments.
Primary key for cooling_process

Foreign key linking to process.process_flow. Business justification: Cooling processes are part of a process flow. This FK links the cooling process to the process flow it belongs to, enabling flow-level cooling process management.
Foreign key linking to process.process_step. Business justification: Cooling processes occur at specific process steps. This FK links the cooling process to the step where it is applied.
Self-referencing FK on cooling_process (supersedes_cooling_process_id)
Pressure of the backside cooling gas in Torr. Controls thermal coupling between the wafer and the electrostatic chuck or susceptor during cooling.
Type of gas used for backside wafer cooling (e.g., helium backside gas for electrostatic chuck cooling). Affects thermal conductance between wafer and chuck.
Process chamber pressure in millitorr during the cooling step. Relevant for vacuum-based cooling processes where heat transfer is pressure-dependent.
Reference number of the process change notification (PCN) or engineering change order (ECO) that introduced or last modified this cooling process definition.
Comma-separated list of equipment tool IDs on which this cooling process is qualified to run. Used for dispatch and scheduling validation until a formal tool-allocation link is established.
Indicates whether this cooling process carries a risk of moisture condensation on the wafer surface, requiring dew-point monitoring or nitrogen purge protocols.
Nominal flow rate of the coolant medium in standard liters per minute (SLM). Governs heat transfer efficiency and uniformity across the wafer surface.

The physical medium used to transfer heat away from the wafer or substrate during the cooling process. Determines material compatibility and contamination risk. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: nitrogen|air|helium|chilled_water|deionized_water|liquid_nitrogen|co2 — promote to reference product]
Nominal coolant delivery pressure in kilopascals (kPa). Out-of-spec pressure can cause non-uniform cooling or equipment damage.
Nominal duration in seconds for the cooling process to bring the wafer from process exit temperature to the target temperature. Used for cycle time planning and scheduling.
Nominal rate of temperature decrease in degrees Celsius per second. Critical for stress management in thin-film layers and preventing wafer warpage.
Minimum acceptable process capability index (Cpk) for the cooling process temperature control. Typically ≥1.33 for production qualification.
Timestamp when this cooling process record was first created in the system. Supports audit trail and data lineage requirements.
Date from which this version of the cooling process definition is authorized for production use. Supports change control and process history tracking.
EHS risk classification for this cooling process based on chemical, cryogenic, and electrical hazard assessment. Drives safety training and PPE requirements.
Indicates whether an electrostatic chuck (ESC) is required to maintain wafer contact and thermal coupling during the cooling process.
Estimated energy consumption in kilowatt-hours per wafer lot for this cooling process. Used for fab energy management, sustainability reporting, and cost modeling.
Class or type of equipment on which this cooling process is executed (e.g., 'Cooling Station', 'Load Lock', 'Cluster Tool Cool Module'). Used for equipment compatibility matching.
Date after which this cooling process version is no longer authorized for production use. Null indicates no planned expiry.
Indicates whether the cooling process uses hazardous gases (e.g., cryogenic nitrogen, CO2) requiring special handling, PPE, and environmental health and safety (EHS) controls.
Expected wafer temperature in degrees Celsius at the start of the cooling process (i.e., exit temperature from the preceding process step). Used to validate thermal budget compliance.
Maximum allowable cooldown duration in seconds before the process is flagged as out-of-spec. Exceeding this value triggers an equipment alarm and process review.
Maximum permissible cooling rate in degrees Celsius per second to prevent thermal shock, wafer cracking, or film delamination.
Scheduled date for the next mandatory requalification of this cooling process. Derived from qualification_date plus requalification_interval_days but stored for scheduling system integration.
Free-text field for process engineers to record observations, known issues, deviations, or supplementary guidance related to this cooling process.
Full name of the process engineer responsible for maintaining and qualifying this cooling process. PII — must be masked in non-production environments per VREQ-050.
Minimum particle size in nanometers counted toward the particle specification. Defines the sensitivity of post-cooling particle inspection.
Maximum allowable particle adder count on the wafer surface after the cooling process. Exceeding this value triggers a process qualification review.
Externally-known alphanumeric code uniquely identifying this cooling process definition within the process engineering library. Used in traveler documents and process specifications.
Detailed narrative description of the cooling process, including its purpose, integration context within the process flow, and any special handling requirements.
Human-readable name of the cooling process (e.g., 'Post-CMP Wafer Cool', 'Post-Etch Rapid Cool'). Used in process flow documentation and operator instructions.
Current lifecycle status of the cooling process definition. Controls whether the process may be scheduled on production equipment.
The semiconductor manufacturing step after which this cooling process is applied (e.g., 'Post-CMP', 'Post-Etch', 'Post-Implant', 'Post-Anneal'). Defines integration context.

Classification of the cooling mechanism employed. Determines equipment compatibility and safety protocols. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: forced_convection|natural_convection|cryogenic|liquid_nitrogen|chilled_water|peltier|radiative — promote to reference product]
Date on which this cooling process was most recently qualified for production use. Used to track qualification currency and schedule requalification.
Current process qualification status indicating whether this cooling process has passed all required qualification tests for production use.
Number of days between mandatory requalification runs for this cooling process. Drives the process maintenance schedule.
Indicates whether safety interlocks (e.g., over-temperature shutoff, gas flow interlock) are mandatory for this cooling process per SEMI S2 safety guidelines.
Type of SPC control chart applied to monitor this cooling process. Determines the statistical method used for process control and out-of-control detection.
Material composition of the wafer substrate for which this cooling process is designed. Different materials have distinct thermal conductivity and expansion coefficients.
Nominal target wafer or substrate temperature in degrees Celsius that the cooling process must achieve before the wafer is released to the next process step.
Semiconductor technology node (e.g., '7nm', '14nm', '28nm') for which this cooling process is qualified. Determines applicability to specific product families.
Lower control limit for wafer temperature in degrees Celsius. Wafers below this limit may indicate over-cooling, triggering condensation risk checks.
Upper control limit for wafer temperature in degrees Celsius. Wafers exceeding this limit trigger a process hold or alarm condition.
Integrated thermal exposure (temperature × time) in degree-Celsius-seconds allocated to this cooling step within the overall process thermal budget. Constrains dopant diffusion and film integrity.
Maximum allowable temperature non-uniformity across the wafer surface expressed as a percentage. Directly impacts device performance and yield.
Timestamp when this cooling process record was last modified. Supports change tracking, audit trail, and incremental data pipeline processing.
Version identifier of this cooling process recipe definition (e.g., '1.0', '2.3'). Tracks process revisions and supports change control traceability.
Nominal wafer diameter in millimeters for which this cooling process is qualified (e.g., 200, 300, 450). Determines applicability to specific fab lines.
Unique identifier for the DOE experiment record. Primary key for the DOE experiment entity.
Foreign key linking to invoice.ar_invoice. Business justification: DOE NRE Billing links a design‑of‑experiments project to the invoice that charges the customer for the NRE work.
Reference to the process engineer responsible for designing, executing, and analyzing the DOE experiment.
FK to fabrication.fab_site
Reference to the specific process step under study in this DOE experiment (e.g., photolithography, etch, deposition, implant).
Reference to the technology node (e.g., 7nm, 5nm, 3nm) for which this DOE experiment is being conducted.
Foreign key linking to product.ic_catalog. Business justification: DOE experiments are conducted for a specific IC product; results feed the DOE Results per Product analysis.

Foreign key linking to process.process_flow. Business justification: DOE experiments are conducted to optimize a specific process flow. This FK links the experiment to the flow being characterized, enabling process flow-level DOE tracking.

Foreign key linking to process.process_qualification. Business justification: DOE experiments are often conducted as part of a process qualification program. This FK links the experiment to the qualification it supports.
FK to process.process_process_step.process_process_step_id — DOE experiments study specific process steps. The step reference identifies what is being optimized.
Actual date when the DOE experiment was completed, including all runs, analysis, and final reporting.
Actual date when the DOE experiment began wafer processing and data collection.
Software tool or platform used for DOE design and statistical analysis (e.g., JMP, Minitab, Design-Expert, R, Python).
Date when the DOE experiment plan or results were formally approved.
Approval status of the DOE experiment plan and results by management or technical review board.
Process capability index (Cpk) measured before the DOE experiment, serving as the baseline for comparison.
Factor used to group experimental runs into blocks to control for known sources of variation (e.g., different lots, different days, different chambers).
Timestamp when the DOE experiment record was first created in the system.

Statistical design methodology used for the experiment. Full factorial explores all factor combinations; fractional factorial reduces runs; RSM optimizes response surfaces; Taguchi focuses on robustness; Plackett-Burman screens many factors efficiently; Central Composite designs for quadratic models.
Business identifier for the DOE experiment, typically a human-readable code used for tracking and reference across engineering teams.
Detailed description of the business and technical objectives of the DOE experiment, including the problem statement, hypothesis, and expected outcomes.

Current lifecycle status of the DOE experiment. Tracks progression from planning through execution to completion. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: planned|in_progress|data_collection|analysis|completed|cancelled|on_hold — 7 candidates stripped; promote to reference product]
Descriptive title of the DOE experiment that summarizes the objective and scope of the study.
Number of independent variables (factors) being studied in the DOE experiment. Each factor represents a process parameter that may influence the response variables.
Description of the levels (values) tested for each factor in the experiment. Typically includes low, medium, and high settings or specific numerical values.
Comma-separated list of factors (independent variables) being studied, such as temperature, pressure, dose, time, gas flow rate, power, etc.
Description of any significant interaction effects between factors, where the effect of one factor depends on the level of another factor.
Summary of the main conclusions from the DOE experiment, including significant factors, interactions, optimal settings, and process insights.
Timestamp when the DOE experiment record was last updated or modified.
Additional notes, observations, or comments related to the DOE experiment, including any deviations from plan, special conditions, or lessons learned.
Engineering organization or department responsible for the DOE experiment (e.g., Process Integration, Lithography, Etch, Thin Films).
Planned date for the DOE experiment to complete all runs, analysis, and reporting.
Planned date for the DOE experiment to begin wafer processing and data collection.
Process capability index (Cpk) measured after implementing the DOE recommendations, demonstrating the improvement achieved.
The main response variable of interest for optimization or characterization in this DOE experiment.
Quantitative or qualitative assessment of process capability improvement achieved through the DOE experiment, such as Cpk improvement, defect reduction, or yield enhancement.
Lower boundary of the acceptable process window for key factors, defining the range within which the process remains stable and meets specifications.
Upper boundary of the acceptable process window for key factors, defining the range within which the process remains stable and meets specifications.
Optimal or recommended process parameter settings derived from the DOE analysis to achieve desired response variable targets.
Number of times each experimental condition is replicated to assess measurement variability and improve statistical power.
Number of dependent variables (responses) being measured in the DOE experiment. Response variables are the outcomes being optimized or characterized.
Comma-separated list of response variables being measured, such as Critical Dimension (CD), uniformity, defect density, yield, thickness, etch rate, etc.
Total number of experimental runs (treatment combinations) planned or executed in the DOE study.
Strategy used to sequence experimental runs. Randomization minimizes bias; blocking controls for known sources of variation; center point replication assesses curvature and repeatability.
List of factors that were statistically significant in affecting the response variables, based on the analysis results.
Statistical techniques applied to analyze the DOE results, such as ANOVA, regression analysis, main effects plots, interaction plots, contour plots, or optimization algorithms.
Total number of wafers allocated to the DOE experiment across all runs and splits.
Description of how wafers are allocated across different experimental conditions, including split assignments, replication strategy, and randomization approach.
Estimated or measured impact on wafer yield resulting from the DOE experiment and implementation of recommended changes, expressed as a percentage change.
Primary key for excursion
Reference to the process engineer responsible for investigating and resolving this excursion. Links to employee or engineer master data.
Reference to the manufacturing equipment where the excursion occurred. Links to equipment master data.
Reference to the specific process step where the excursion was detected. Links to process step master data.
Reference to the technology node of the affected product. Links to technology node master data.
Reference to the primary wafer lot affected by this excursion. Links to the fabrication wafer lot master data.
Foreign key linking to product.ic_catalog. Business justification: Excursions are recorded per IC product to assess impact; required for the Excursion Impact Report.

Foreign key linking to process.process_flow. Business justification: Excursions occur within a specific process flow context. This FK enables process flow-level excursion reporting and yield impact analysis.

Foreign key linking to process.process_step. Business justification: Process excursions occur at specific process steps. This FK is critical for root cause analysis and step-level excursion tracking. No existing FK from excursion to process_step.
Foreign key linking to research.project. Business justification: Excursions on wafers derived from a research project must be logged to the project for risk tracking and corrective action.

Foreign key linking to process.spc_control_chart. Business justification: Excursions are often detected via SPC chart violations. Linking excursion to the triggering SPC chart enables traceability from excursion back to the monitoring chart.
Estimated total number of die units affected by this excursion. Used for financial impact assessment and customer notification.
Total number of wafer lots affected by this excursion event. Used for impact assessment and containment scope determination.
Total number of individual wafers affected by this excursion across all lots. Used for yield impact calculation.
Immediate containment actions taken upon excursion detection. Describes steps such as lot hold, equipment quarantine, process parameter adjustment, or rework initiation.
Date and time when containment actions were completed. Used to measure response time and containment effectiveness.
Reference number or identifier for the corrective action plan. Links to the formal corrective and preventive action (CAPA) system or Out-of-Control Action Plan (OCAP) record.
Date and time when this excursion record was first created in the system. Audit trail for record creation.
Flag indicating whether customer notification is required for this excursion. True if the excursion impacts shipped product or customer-specific quality requirements.
Date and time when customer was notified of the excursion. Used for compliance tracking and customer relationship management.
Measured defect density in defects per square centimeter for the affected wafers. Key metric for yield impact assessment.

Source system or method that detected the excursion event. Indicates whether the excursion was identified through Statistical Process Control (SPC), inspection, metrology measurement, electrical test, operator observation, or automated monitoring system.
Date and time when the excursion was first detected. Represents the business event time of excursion identification.
Final disposition decision for the affected material. Determines whether lots are released, reworked, scrapped, returned, or require additional review.
Date and time when the final disposition decision was made. Marks the completion of the excursion investigation and material release decision.
Estimated financial impact of the excursion in US dollars. Includes scrap cost, rework cost, yield loss, and potential customer claims.
Estimated percentage point impact on wafer yield due to this excursion. Calculated based on defect density, affected area, and historical correlation data.
Business identifier for the excursion event. Human-readable reference number used for tracking and communication across engineering teams.

Classification of the excursion event. Categorizes whether the excursion is due to out-of-specification parameters, out-of-control process conditions, yield loss, excessive defect density, parametric drift, or equipment fault.
Current lifecycle status of the excursion investigation. Tracks progression from initial detection through root cause analysis to closure.
Date and time when this excursion record was last updated. Audit trail for tracking investigation progress and status changes.
Lower control limit from the Statistical Process Control (SPC) chart at the time of excursion detection. Used to determine out-of-control conditions.
Actual measured value of the parameter that triggered the excursion. Used for root cause analysis and process capability assessment.
Additional notes, comments, or observations related to the excursion investigation. Captures engineering insights and lessons learned.
Engineering organization or department responsible for this excursion. Examples include Process Integration, Lithography, Etch, Thin Films, or Metrology.
Name of the process parameter that triggered the excursion. Examples include critical dimension (CD), film thickness, overlay, resistance, or other measured characteristics.

High-level classification of the root cause. Categories include equipment malfunction, material variation, process recipe issue, human error, environmental condition, or unknown pending investigation.
Detailed description of the identified root cause. Captures engineering analysis findings and technical explanation of the excursion mechanism.

Severity classification of the excursion impact. Minor excursions require monitoring, major excursions require corrective action, and critical excursions require immediate containment and investigation.
Specific Statistical Process Control (SPC) rule that was violated to trigger the excursion. Examples include Western Electric rules, Nelson rules, or custom fab-specific control rules.
Lower specification limit for the parameter. Used to determine out-of-specification conditions and product quality impact.
Upper specification limit for the parameter. Used to determine out-of-specification conditions and product quality impact.
Target specification value for the parameter. Used to calculate deviation magnitude and assess excursion severity.
Unit of measure for the parameter values. Examples include nanometers (nm), angstroms, ohms, degrees Celsius, or other engineering units.
Upper control limit from the Statistical Process Control (SPC) chart at the time of excursion detection. Used to determine out-of-control conditions.

Classification of the yield loss mechanism. Parametric indicates parameter drift, systematic indicates repeatable pattern defects, random indicates sporadic defects, electrical indicates functional test failures, none indicates no yield impact detected.

Foreign key linking to customer.account. Business justification: Needed for Order Traceability: lot process runs are executed for a specific customer account, enabling billing, compliance, and yield reporting per customer.

Foreign key linking to finance.cost_center. Business justification: MES cost accounting assigns each lot run to a cost center for budgeting and variance analysis, required for daily production cost reporting.
Reference to the fab operator who initiated or supervised this process run. Used for training effectiveness analysis and operator certification tracking.

Foreign key linking to research.experimental_lot. Business justification: Production tracking of research experimental lots is required for yield learning and cost accounting; linking run to experimental lot enables this.
Reference to the specific fabrication equipment tool that processed this lot. Critical for equipment performance analysis, utilization tracking, and SPC (Statistical Process Control) correlation.
Reference to the specific process step in the technology node process flow that this lot executed. Defines the operation type (photolithography, etch, deposition, implant, CMP, metrology, etc.).
Reference to the wafer lot that executed this process step. Links to the fabrication domain lot master record for WIP (Work in Process) tracking and genealogy.

Foreign key linking to process.inspection_point. Business justification: Lot process runs may be inspected at defined inspection points. This FK links the run to the inspection point where quality checks occur.

FK to process.process_process_recipe.process_process_recipe_id — Each lot run uses a specific recipe version. Critical for traceability - which recipe was actually used for a given lot at a given step.
Foreign key linking to order.order. Business justification: Required for Order Fulfillment Tracking report that ties each production run to the sales order it fulfills.

Reference to the actual recipe executed for this process step. May differ from the planned recipe if engineering overrides or recipe revisions occurred. Links to the recipe master in the fabrication domain.

Foreign key linking to process.process_flow. Business justification: A lot process run executes within a specific process flow. This FK is critical for tracing lot execution back to the process flow definition.
FK to process.process_process_step.process_process_step_id — Every lot process run is an execution of a specific process step. This FK is essential for WIP tracking and SPC data collection context.

Foreign key linking to process.sampling_plan. Business justification: Lot process runs follow a sampling plan that governs measurement frequency and inspection criteria. This FK links the run to the governing sampling plan.
Foreign key linking to workforce.shift_schedule. Business justification: Labor costing and compliance audits require linking each lot run to the shift schedule that covered it.
Foreign key linking to product.sku. Business justification: Lot Traceability report ties each run to the SKU being produced, enabling yield and compliance tracking.

Foreign key linking to process.spc_control_chart. Business justification: Lot process runs are monitored by SPC control charts. The control_chart_violation_flag and control_chart_rule_violated fields indicate this relationship. This FK links the run to the monitoring chart.
Identifier of the specific chamber or module within the equipment tool where the lot was processed. Critical for multi-chamber tools to isolate chamber-specific process variation and matching.
Actual date and time when the lot processing completed on the equipment. Used to calculate actual process duration and equipment throughput.
Actual date and time when the lot processing began on the equipment. Captured from MES equipment interface for cycle time analysis and equipment utilization tracking.

business_glossary_term: SPC (Statistical Process Control) Control Chart Rule Violated
description: Specific SPC rule violated if control_chart_violation_flag is true (e.g., 'Western Electric Rule 1: Point beyond 3-sigma', 'Nelson Rule 2: Nine points in a row on same side of centerline'). Null if no violation.

business_glossary_term: SPC (Statistical Process Control) Control Chart Violation Flag
description: Boolean flag indicating whether this process run violated SPC control chart rules (e.g., out-of-control limits, run rules, trend rules). True indicates a violation requiring engineering investigation.

Standardized code indicating the reason for lot hold if lot_disposition is 'hold'. Examples include out-of-spec measurement, equipment alarm, material shortage, engineering investigation. Null if lot not on hold.

Outcome disposition of the lot after completing this process step. Measured temperature during/after cooling.

Primary in-line measurement result collected at this process step (e.g., film thickness, CD (Critical Dimension), overlay, resistivity). The specific measurement type is defined by the process step. Used for real-time SPC and process control.
Number of measurement sites sampled on the wafer(s) for this in-line measurement. Used to assess measurement sampling adequacy and statistical confidence.

Unit of measure for the in-line measurement result. Actual elapsed time in seconds for this process run, calculated from start to end timestamp. Used for cycle time analysis, equipment performance monitoring, and throughput optimization.

business_glossary_term: Process Gas Flow in Standard Cubic Centimeters per Minute (SCCM)
description: Total process gas flow rate in standard cubic centimeters per minute (SCCM) for this run. Aggregated flow for multi-gas processes; individual gas flows tracked in separate metrology records.
Free-text notes entered by the operator or process engineer documenting any anomalies, deviations, or special conditions during this process run. Used for root cause analysis and process improvement.
Actual RF or DC power in watts applied during this process run. Key parameter for plasma-based processes including etch, PVD, and PECVD (Plasma-Enhanced Chemical Vapor Deposition).
Actual process chamber pressure in Torr recorded during this run. Critical parameter for vacuum processes including PVD (Physical Vapor Deposition), CVD, etch, and implant.

Qualification status of the process step execution. Qualified indicates the process met all capability and stability requirements; not_qualified indicates process failed qualification criteria; pending_qualification indicates qualification in progress; requalification_required indicates process drift detected requiring re-qualification.
Actual process temperature in degrees Celsius recorded during this run. Key process parameter for thermal processes such as CVD (Chemical Vapor Deposition), diffusion, annealing, and oxidation.
Version identifier of the recipe executed. Critical for process change control and correlation of recipe revisions to yield and quality outcomes.
Timestamp when this process run record was first created in the source MES system. Used for data lineage and audit trail purposes.
Timestamp when this process run record was last updated in the source MES system. Tracks modifications to disposition, measurements, or other attributes after initial creation.
Number of times this lot has been reworked at this process step. Tracks process stability and identifies chronic problem areas. Zero indicates first-pass processing.
Business identifier for this process run event. Typically a sequential or timestamp-based identifier assigned by the MES (Manufacturing Execution System) for traceability and audit purposes.

Standardized code indicating the reason for lot scrap if lot_disposition is 'scrap'. Examples include catastrophic defect, process excursion, contamination, equipment failure. Null if lot not scrapped.
Number of wafers in the lot that were processed in this run. May differ from the original lot size if wafers were scrapped or held at prior steps.
Waste elimination strategy applied.
Whether waste heat was recovered.

Reference to the manufacturing equipment where the process excursion was detected.
Reference to the specific process step where the SPC excursion occurred and triggered the OCAP action.
Reference to the technology node associated with the process flow where the excursion occurred.

Foreign key linking to process.process_step. Business justification: OCAP actions are triggered at specific process steps where SPC violations or excursions occur. This FK enables step-level OCAP tracking and corrective action analysis.
FK to process.spc_measurement.spc_measurement_id — OCAP actions are triggered by specific SPC rule violations on measurements. This link connects the corrective action to its triggering event.

Final disposition decision for the affected wafer lot after OCAP action completion (e.g., released to next step, held for further investigation, scrapped).
Additional notes, comments, or observations related to the OCAP action that provide context for future reference and continuous improvement.

Method used to verify the effectiveness of the corrective action (e.g., SPC monitoring, metrology inspection, yield analysis).

Flag indicating whether this qualification requires formal customer approval before production deployment. True for customer-specific processes or foundry qualifications.

Rotational speed of the wafer carrier head during CMP processing, measured in revolutions per minute. Affects relative velocity and material removal uniformity.
Detailed textual description of this CMP process condition, including special considerations, application scope, and any unique characteristics or constraints.

Classification of the CMP process step based on the material and layer being planarized. Unique alphanumeric code assigned to this CMP condition for tracking and reference in Manufacturing Execution System (MES) and process control systems.
Human-readable name or identifier for this CMP process condition, typically including the CMP step type and target layer.
Type of diamond conditioning disk or brush used to maintain pad surface texture and porosity during CMP processing. Conditioning prevents pad glazing and maintains consistent removal rates.
Schedule for pad conditioning operations, such as ex-situ (between wafers), in-situ (during polishing), or time-based intervals. Maintains pad performance and removal rate stability.
Coolant flow rate in liters per minute.
Whether coolant is recirculated to reduce waste.
Percentage of coolant reclaimed/recycled.
Volume of coolant waste generated.

Controlled cooling ramp-down rate.
Target wafer/chamber temperature after cooling.
