Maximum allowable number of CMP-induced defects per wafer, including scratches, particles, residue, and surface damage. Measured by post-CMP inspection tools.
Maximum allowable depression or dishing in metal features (copper lines, tungsten plugs) after CMP, measured in angstroms. Excessive dishing degrades electrical performance and reliability.
Date when this CMP condition version was superseded or retired from production use. Null for currently active conditions.
Date when this CMP condition version became active and available for production use.

Technology used to determine when the target material removal amount has been achieved. Optical methods detect reflectivity changes, motor current monitors torque variations, time-based uses fixed duration, acoustic detects friction changes, eddy current senses metal thickness.
Classification of CMP tool type qualified for this condition, such as rotary single-wafer, linear, or orbital polisher. Different tool architectures may require condition adjustments.
Maximum allowable thinning of dielectric material in dense pattern areas relative to isolated areas, measured in angstroms. Erosion affects metal line height uniformity and interconnect reliability.
Identifier of the fabrication facility where this CMP condition is qualified and used. Enables multi-site process tracking and technology transfer.
Indicates whether this is the baseline or reference CMP condition for this step type and technology node. Baseline conditions serve as the standard for process capability and yield comparisons.
Timestamp when this CMP condition record was most recently updated or modified.
Additional notes, comments, or observations related to this CMP condition, including troubleshooting guidance, known issues, or optimization opportunities.
Engineering organization or department responsible for maintaining and updating this CMP process condition.
Commercial product name or part number of the CMP polishing pad as designated by the vendor.
Classification and material composition of the CMP polishing pad, including hardness, porosity, groove pattern, and surface texture characteristics that affect removal rate and uniformity.
Manufacturer or supplier of the CMP polishing pad used in this process condition.
Downforce pressure applied by the wafer carrier to the polishing pad surface, measured in pounds per square inch. Critical parameter controlling removal rate and within-wafer uniformity.
Target duration of the CMP polishing step in seconds, from wafer contact with pad to endpoint detection or time-based completion.

Cleaning process recipe applied immediately after CMP to remove slurry residue, particles, and contaminants from the wafer surface. Typically includes brush scrubbing, chemical rinse, and DI water rinse steps.
Manufacturing layer classification indicating whether this CMP step occurs in Front End of Line (FEOL), Middle of Line (MOL), or Back End of Line (BEOL) processing.
Date when this CMP condition successfully completed qualification testing and was approved for production use.

Current lifecycle state of this CMP condition in the process qualification workflow. Draft conditions are under development, under qualification are being validated, qualified have passed testing, production approved are released for volume manufacturing, suspended are temporarily disabled, obsolete are retired.
Name of the process engineer responsible for qualifying and approving this CMP condition for production use.
Expected rate of material removal during CMP processing, measured in angstroms per minute. Determined by slurry chemistry, pad type, pressure, and speed parameters.

Ratio of removal rates between the target material and underlying stop layer or adjacent materials. High selectivity ensures controlled polishing without excessive removal of barrier or dielectric layers.

Chemical composition classification of the CMP slurry used in this condition, including abrasive type (silica, ceria, alumina), pH level, and chemical additives for selectivity and removal rate control.
Target volumetric flow rate of CMP slurry delivered to the polishing pad surface during processing, measured in milliliters per minute.
Commercial product name or part number of the CMP slurry as designated by the vendor.
Manufacturer or supplier of the CMP slurry chemical used in this process condition.
Rotational speed of the CMP platen table holding the polishing pad, measured in revolutions per minute. Combined with carrier speed determines relative motion and removal characteristics.
Specific layer or film stack being planarized by this CMP process, such as oxide, nitride, metal layer designation (M1, M2, etc.), or via layer.
Specified thickness of material to be removed during the CMP process, measured in angstroms. Critical for achieving target post-CMP film thickness and planarity.
Version identifier for this CMP condition record, incremented when process parameters are modified. Enables change tracking and process history analysis.
Maximum allowable variation in average material removal between wafers processed under this condition, expressed as a percentage. Ensures consistent lot-level processing.
Waste elimination strategy (e.g., heat_recovery, coolant_reclaim, lean_idle_shutdown).
Whether waste heat is recovered/reused.
Maximum allowable variation in material removal across the wafer surface, expressed as a percentage. Calculated as (max - min) / (2 * mean) * 100. Critical for device performance uniformity.

Automated or manual action triggered when the cooling condition falls outside its control limits. Defines the response protocol for out-of-spec cooling events.
Required ambient temperature in degrees Celsius within the process module or mini-environment during the cooling step. Ensures environmental control for sensitive process nodes.
Date on which this cooling condition was formally approved by the responsible engineer or process owner for production deployment.
Full name of the process or quality engineer who formally approved this cooling condition for production use. PII — masked in non-production environments.
Process chamber pressure in Torr during the cooling step. Vacuum or controlled-atmosphere conditions affect the cooling rate and prevent contamination.
Narrative description of the reason for creating or modifying this cooling condition. Supports process change management and audit trail requirements.
Set-point temperature of the electrostatic or mechanical chuck in degrees Celsius during the cooling step. Controls wafer backside heat transfer and uniformity.
ISO cleanroom classification required for performing this cooling step. Ensures the cooling environment meets contamination control requirements for the target technology node.

Externally-known alphanumeric code uniquely identifying this cooling condition within the process engineering system. Used as a human-readable reference across process flows, equipment recipes, and qualification documents.
Detailed narrative description of the cooling condition, including its purpose within the process flow, applicable process steps, and any special handling notes.
Descriptive name for the cooling condition (e.g., 'Post-Etch Rapid Cool', 'Diffusion Furnace Ramp-Down'). Used in process documentation, traveler sheets, and operator interfaces.

Classification of the cooling method applied. Determines the thermal profile and equipment configuration required. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: rapid_cool|slow_cool|controlled_ramp|natural_convection|forced_convection|cryogenic — promote to reference product if additional types are needed]
Nominal flow rate of the cooling medium in standard liters per minute (SLM). Directly impacts cooling efficiency and uniformity across the wafer surface.
The cooling medium used to achieve the required thermal profile (e.g., nitrogen purge, forced air, helium backside cooling, chilled water). Determines equipment compatibility and safety requirements.
Pressure of the cooling medium in Torr, particularly relevant for helium backside cooling in vacuum process chambers. Affects thermal conductance between wafer and chuck.
Maximum allowable cooling duration in seconds before the wafer is flagged for review. Exceeding this limit may indicate equipment degradation or abnormal thermal load.
Nominal duration in seconds for the cooling step to reach the target temperature. Used for cycle time planning and equipment scheduling.
The nominal rate at which temperature is reduced, expressed in degrees Celsius per minute. Controls thermal stress on the wafer and prevents crystal defects or film delamination.
Maximum allowable cooling rate in degrees Celsius per minute. Exceeding this rate risks thermal shock, wafer warpage, or film stress failures.

Timestamp when this cooling condition record was first created in the system. Used for audit trail and data lineage tracking.
Date from which this cooling condition becomes effective in production process flows. May differ from approval date if a staged rollout is planned.
Type of semiconductor processing equipment for which this cooling condition is defined (e.g., diffusion furnace, RTP system, etch chamber, CVD reactor, track system). Ensures recipe compatibility.
Maximum allowable relative humidity percentage in the cooling environment. Excessive humidity can cause condensation on cooled wafers, leading to defects.
Indicates whether this cooling condition is currently active and available for use in process recipes. Inactive conditions are retained for historical traceability but cannot be assigned to new lots.
Indicates whether this is the default cooling condition for the associated process step and technology node. Used by recipe management systems to auto-assign conditions when no override is specified.
Method used to measure wafer or chuck temperature during the cooling step (e.g., thermocouple, pyrometer, RTD, infrared sensor). Determines measurement accuracy and calibration requirements.
Location on the wafer or equipment where the temperature is measured to determine cooling completion (e.g., wafer center, wafer edge, chuck surface, ambient).
Date on which this cooling condition was or will be retired from active use. Retained for historical traceability and audit purposes.
Full name of the process engineer responsible for defining, maintaining, and qualifying this cooling condition. PII — masked in non-production environments per VREQ-050.
Maximum allowable airborne particle count per cubic meter during the cooling step. Exceeding this limit may cause wafer contamination and yield loss.
Reference number of the Process Change Notification (PCN) that introduced or last modified this cooling condition. Links to the formal change management record.

The semiconductor manufacturing process step to which this cooling condition applies (e.g., photolithography, etch, diffusion, implant, deposition, metrology). Aligns the cooling condition to the correct process module.
Date on which this cooling condition was formally qualified for production use. Marks the start of the valid production window.
Current qualification status of this cooling condition for production use. Only 'qualified' conditions may be used in production process flows; others require engineering approval.
Version identifier of the process recipe in which this cooling condition is embedded. Supports recipe change management and traceability to qualification data.
Date by which this cooling condition must be requalified to remain valid for production use. Driven by process change notification (PCN) schedules or periodic review cycles.
Indicates whether a safety interlock must be engaged during this cooling step (e.g., chamber door lock, gas flow interlock). Ensures compliance with fab safety protocols.
Code identifying the originating process engineering or manufacturing execution system (MES) from which this cooling condition record was sourced (e.g., Camstar, WorkStream, Promis).

Required dwell time in seconds after reaching the target temperature to ensure thermal equilibrium before the wafer is transferred to the next process step.

The semiconductor technology node (e.g., 28nm, 14nm, 7nm, 5nm) for which this cooling condition is qualified. Ensures process compatibility with the target device geometry.
Lower control limit for the cooling endpoint temperature in degrees Celsius. Wafers below this limit may indicate over-cooling, which can cause condensation or thermal stress defects.
Upper control limit for the cooling endpoint temperature in degrees Celsius. Wafers exceeding this limit must be re-cooled or placed on hold per SPC rules.
Integrated thermal budget for the cooling step expressed in degree-Celsius-seconds (°C·s). Used to track cumulative thermal exposure and ensure device junction integrity.
Maximum allowable temperature non-uniformity across the wafer surface at the end of the cooling step, expressed as a percentage. Critical for within-wafer process uniformity and yield.
Timestamp of the most recent modification to this cooling condition record. Used for change tracking, SPC monitoring, and data freshness validation.
Substrate material for which this cooling condition is applicable. Different materials have distinct thermal conductivity and expansion coefficients requiring tailored cooling profiles.
Nominal wafer diameter in millimeters for which this cooling condition is defined (e.g., 200, 300, 450). Cooling parameters are wafer-size-dependent due to thermal mass differences.

Date when this etch condition becomes effective and available for production use. Format: yyyy-MM-dd.
Method used to detect the completion of the etch process. Options include time-based, Optical Emission Spectroscopy (OES), laser interferometry, mass spectrometry, or none for over-etch strategies.
Specific etch method or technology used. For dry etch: Reactive Ion Etch (RIE), Inductively Coupled Plasma (ICP), Capacitively Coupled Plasma (CCP). For wet etch: immersion or spray.
Target etch rate for the primary material in angstroms per minute. Key process parameter for throughput and uniformity control.
Duration of the etch process in seconds. May be time-based or endpoint-detection-based depending on the process control strategy.
Classification of the etch process as either dry etch (plasma-based: RIE, ICP, CCP) or wet etch (chemical immersion).
The chemical composition of the etchant used. For dry etch: gas mixture (e.g., CF4/O2, SF6/O2, Cl2/BCl3). For wet etch: liquid chemistry (e.g., HF, BOE, H3PO4).
Code identifying the fabrication facility where this etch condition is qualified and used. Supports multi-site manufacturing operations.
Boolean flag indicating whether this is the baseline or reference etch condition for the process step. True if this is the standard condition, False otherwise.

Free-text field for additional notes, comments, or special instructions related to the etch condition. Used for operational guidance and knowledge capture.
Percentage of additional etch time beyond endpoint detection to ensure complete material removal. Expressed as a percentage of the main etch time.
Organizational unit or department responsible for this etch condition (e.g., FEOL Process Engineering, BEOL Integration).
Flow rate of the primary etch gas in standard cubic centimeters per minute (SCCM). Applicable to dry etch processes.
Classification of the etch step within the fabrication flow: Front End of Line (FEOL) for gate patterning, Middle of Line (MOL) for contact/via etch, or Back End of Line (BEOL) for metal etch.
Target sidewall angle of the etched feature in degrees from vertical. 90 degrees indicates perfectly vertical (anisotropic) etch. Critical for device performance and yield.
Date when the etch condition was formally qualified and approved for production use. Format: yyyy-MM-dd.
Current qualification and approval status of the etch condition. Indicates whether the condition is ready for production use or still under development.
Version identifier for the etch recipe. Used for change control and traceability when recipe parameters are updated.
RF power applied to the substrate electrode (bias power) in watts. Controls ion energy and directionality for anisotropic etching.
RF power applied to the plasma source coil or electrode in watts. Controls plasma density and etch rate in ICP and CCP systems.
Flow rate of the secondary or carrier gas in standard cubic centimeters per minute (SCCM). Used for process tuning and selectivity control.
Ratio of etch rate of the target material to the etch rate of the underlying or masking material. Critical for process control and preventing over-etch damage.
Temperature of the wafer substrate during the etch process in degrees Celsius. Affects etch rate, selectivity, and profile control.
Target critical dimension after etch in nanometers. Defines the intended feature size for the etched structure.

Classification or family of etch equipment for which this condition is designed (e.g., Applied Materials Centura, Lam Research Kiyo, Tokyo Electron Tactras).
Target uniformity of etch depth or CD across the wafer, expressed as a percentage (1-sigma or 3-sigma). Lower values indicate better uniformity.
Waste elimination strategy (e.g., heat_recovery, coolant_reclaim, lean_idle_shutdown).
Whether waste heat is recovered/reused.

Total number of ions implanted per square centimeter of wafer surface. Expressed in scientific notation (e.g., 1.0E15 ions/cm²). Controls the dopant concentration.
Ion implantation energy in kilo-electron volts (keV). Controls the depth of ion penetration into the silicon substrate. Typical range: 0.5 keV to 500 keV.
Name of the device layer being implanted (e.g., PWELL, NWELL, LDD, HDD, VT_ADJUST). Identifies the target region in the device structure.

Functional purpose of this implant step in the device structure. Classification of ion implanter equipment type required for this condition. Different tool classes are optimized for specific energy and dose ranges.
Specific model or type of ion implanter equipment qualified for this condition (e.g., Applied Materials Quantum X, Axcelis Purion).
Boolean flag indicating whether this is the baseline or reference implant condition for this layer and technology node. True if baseline, false otherwise.
Timestamp when this implant condition record was most recently updated. Follows format yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ss.SSSXXX. Used for change tracking and audit purposes.
Additional technical notes, warnings, or special instructions for manufacturing operators and process engineers regarding this implant condition.
Name of the engineering organization or department responsible for this implant condition (e.g., Ion Implant Process Engineering, FEOL Integration).
Allowable variation in implant dose as a percentage of target dose, defining the process window for dose control. Typical values: ±1% to ±5%.
Allowable variation in implant energy as a percentage of target energy, defining the process window for energy control. Typical values: ±1% to ±3%.
Date when this implant condition successfully completed qualification testing and was approved for production use.
Current qualification and release status of this implant condition. Indicates whether the condition is approved for production use or still under development.
Boolean flag indicating whether a pre-amorphization implant (typically germanium or silicon) is required before the main dopant implant to reduce channeling effects.
Intended electrical junction depth in nanometers after implant and anneal. Critical specification for transistor performance and short-channel effect control.
Target electrical sheet resistance in ohms per square after implant and anneal. Key electrical parameter for verifying dopant activation and concentration.
Target transistor threshold voltage in millivolts that this implant condition is designed to achieve. Critical for device performance and power consumption.
Angle of wafer tilt relative to the ion beam axis, measured in degrees. Used to control channeling effects and implant profile symmetry. Typical range: 0° to 7°.
Rotational angle of the wafer around its normal axis during implantation, measured in degrees. Used in conjunction with tilt angle to optimize implant uniformity. Typical range: 0° to 360°.
Version identifier for this implant condition. Incremented when process parameters are modified. Enables change tracking and configuration management.

Foreign key linking to process.process_flow. Business justification: Yield loss events occur within a specific process flow. This FK enables process flow-level yield loss reporting and optimization.

Foreign key linking to process.process_step. Business justification: Yield loss events are identified at specific process steps. This FK is critical for step-level yield loss analysis and root cause investigation.
Reference to the process recipe or run card that was active when the yield loss event occurred. Used for recipe-specific yield correlation and optimization.
Foreign key linking to research.project. Business justification: Yield loss events on research wafers are reported to the responsible research project for root‑cause analysis and budget impact.
Reference to the IC design product or device family being fabricated on the affected wafer lot. Used for product-specific yield analysis and customer impact assessment.

Foreign key linking to process.spc_control_chart. Business justification: Yield loss events are often triggered by SPC rule violations. The spc_rule_violation field on yield_loss_event indicates this relationship. Linking to the SPC chart enables traceability.
Number of individual die on the wafer affected by this yield loss event. Used to calculate yield impact and prioritize corrective actions.
Name or identifier of the engineer or team assigned to investigate and resolve the yield loss event. Used for workload management and escalation.
Coolant flow rate observed.
Coolant reclaim percentage.
Coolant waste volume generated.
Duration of cooling observed.
Energy consumed by cooling.
Cooling method applied.
Whether cooling optimization was active.
Cooling optimization option in effect.
Indicates whether a cooling process is associated.
Measured cooling ramp-down rate.

Process capability index (Cpk) calculated at the time of the yield loss event. Indicates how well the process is centered and controlled relative to specification limits. Values below 1.33 typically indicate process capability issues.
Date and time when this yield loss event record was first created in the system. Format: yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ss.SSSXXX. Used for audit trail and data lineage.
Measured defect density for this yield loss event, expressed as defects per square centimeter of wafer area. Used for SPC trending and yield modeling.
Measured or estimated size of the defect in nanometers. Critical for determining defect criticality and kill ratio at advanced technology nodes.
Specific defect type or signature identified during inspection or test. Examples include scratch, particle, void, short, open, bridging, residue, etc. May reference defect classification taxonomy.

Method or technique used to detect the yield loss event. Indicates whether detected via inline inspection, offline inspection, electrical test, parametric test, visual inspection, or metrology measurement.

Disposition decision for the affected wafer lot following yield loss event investigation. Continue indicates lot proceeds to next step; rework indicates reprocessing required; scrap indicates lot rejected; quarantine indicates lot held pending further analysis; use as is indicates lot accepted with deviation; return to vendor indicates supplier material issue.
Estimated percentage point impact on wafer yield attributable to this event. Calculated from affected die count and total die per wafer. Used for yield loss Pareto analysis.
Date and time when the yield loss event was detected or recorded. Format: yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ss.SSSXXX.
Code identifying the fabrication facility where the yield loss event occurred. Used for multi-site yield benchmarking and site-specific trending.
Date and time when root cause investigation of the yield loss event was initiated. Format: yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ss.SSSXXX.
Date and time when this yield loss event record was last updated. Format: yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ss.SSSXXX. Used for audit trail and change tracking.
Name of the process layer or mask level at which the yield loss event occurred. Examples include M1, M2, POLY, CONTACT, VIA1, etc. Aligns with GDS layer naming convention.
Indicates whether a lot hold was placed on the affected wafer lot as a result of this yield loss event. True if hold applied; false if no hold applied.
Measured temperature during/after cooling.
Free-form text field for additional comments, observations, or context related to the yield loss event. May include investigation findings, lessons learned, or special handling instructions.
Name or identifier of the engineer, operator, or automated system that reported or logged the yield loss event. Used for accountability and follow-up.

Current status of the yield loss event resolution workflow. Open indicates newly detected; under investigation indicates active RCA; root cause identified indicates RCA complete; corrective action implemented indicates fix deployed; closed indicates event resolved; deferred indicates event accepted or postponed.
Date and time when the yield loss event was resolved and closed. Format: yyyy-MM-ddTHH:mm:ss.SSSXXX. Used to calculate mean time to resolution (MTTR) for yield events.

High-level classification of the root cause of the yield loss event. Equipment indicates tool-related issues; material indicates incoming material defects; process indicates recipe or parameter issues; human indicates operator error; environmental indicates cleanroom or facility issues; design indicates layout or design rule issues.
Detailed narrative description of the identified or suspected root cause of the yield loss event. Populated during root cause analysis and investigation.

Business severity classification of the yield loss event based on yield impact, affected volume, and customer impact. Critical indicates immediate action required; major indicates significant yield impact; minor indicates localized impact; negligible indicates minimal business impact.

Identification of the SPC control chart rule that was violated and triggered this yield loss event. Examples include Western Electric rules, Nelson rules, or custom SPC rules. Used to link yield events to process control violations.
X-coordinate position on the wafer where the yield loss event was detected, in millimeters from wafer center. Used for spatial yield analysis and wafer mapping.
Y-coordinate position on the wafer where the yield loss event was detected, in millimeters from wafer center. Used for spatial yield analysis and wafer mapping.
Waste elimination strategy applied.
Whether waste heat was recovered.

Classification of the yield loss mechanism. Parametric indicates out-of-spec electrical parameters; systematic indicates repeatable defect patterns; random indicates sporadic defects; electrical indicates functional failures; particle indicates contamination; pattern indicates lithography or etch issues; edge indicates wafer edge exclusion. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: parametric|systematic|random|electrical|particle|pattern|edge — 7 candidates stripped; promote to reference product]

Unit of measure for the measurement value. Examples include volts (V), amperes (A), ohms (Ω), farads (F), ohms per square (Ω/sq), atoms per cubic centimeter (atoms/cm³), nanometers (nm).

Primary numerical result of the characterization measurement. For scalar measurements (threshold voltage, sheet resistance, leakage current), this is the measured value. For curve measurements (IV, CV), this may represent a key extracted parameter.

Percentage of dies that meet all parametric specifications (timing, power, voltage, current). Subset of die yield focusing on performance parameters within specification limits.

Lower control limit or specification limit for the measured parameter. Used to determine pass/fail status and process capability. Part of Statistical Process Control (SPC) framework.
Upper control limit or specification limit for the measured parameter. Used to determine pass/fail status and process capability. Part of Statistical Process Control (SPC) framework.
Measured improvement in yield percentage achieved after implementing corrective actions. Quantifies the effectiveness of yield learning and process optimization efforts.
Identified root cause or mechanism that is limiting yield performance. Examples include random defects, systematic pattern issues, parametric drift, edge exclusion, or specific process step failures.
